text: Yeah, so the Android Orchestrator is a component that essentially guarantees that every instrumentation test when you turn on Orchestrator.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: is executed in its own process.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And this came out of an observation that we made when we were writing infrastructure that runs tests, all of the tests at Google, which is millions and millions of tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so you see these maybe less frequent occurrences of flakiness that you would maybe not normally see in other companies.

text: And again, Tom Kinch, our tech lead, noticed that there's a lot of statefulness around Android processes that run on Android.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so if, well, one very easy one, of course, if a test crashes, then the entire process goes down.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: and then the remainder of the tests don't don't finish so that's kind of an easy one uh but then there's other i think more more tricky things where state builds up and can impact downstream tests and so at that point he kind of said that and this is especially true i believe of when you launch the android application so when you're launching and doing ui testing
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And at that point, it became clear that we need some sort of process level isolation where every single test method is launched in its own process.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So, you know, to put it in very practical terms, that's the adb shell am instrument command for every single test method versus, you know, adb shell am instrument command for just, say, a test class or the entire test suite.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So all of these architectural decisions then just come in and they naturally, I think, force you to make the correct decisions if you want to have reliable, fast tests.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So I think that's why I'm a big advocate for developers also owning testing.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Testing is not just for regression, it's really a development tool.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The question I have, I think a lot of people are going to have that are listeners of the show is, they may be interested in testing, they have an application, and maybe they're in a very similar situation that you were in when you joined Slack.

text: There was no...

text: Maybe no test there.

text: If you have mentioned a few things, you know, like make sure proper layering, maybe dependency injection and stuff like that.

text: I don't have any tests.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: What would you recommend?

text: Would it be, you know, make sure you have dependency injection, make sure you're using this type of layering.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Yeah, I think the if people want the blog post that I mentioned, this is on the Slack engineering blog kind of goes into most of the stuff in detail.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But I think the important layering is so anything that really.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Well, here's how I would start.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And this is exactly how I started.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I wrote a simple espresso test that launched the say, like the settings screen of your application.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Most applications have a setting screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The setting screen is usually behind the login.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so when you start writing this test, you just you think, OK, this is going to work and you just launch it and you see the login screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then you start saying, oh, what launches that login screen?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: How do I actually get to the settings screen directly without having to go through the login process?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Then you start digging through the dependency injection graph, if you have one.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Well, you always have one.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And you start saying, oh, this component is what's launching the screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So how can I swap out this component and make it believe that we are in a logged in state?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So then you swap that out.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Hopefully you have dagger already and it's or some sort of dependency injection and it's easy to do so.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you set up the sort of the that dependency and configure it properly.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then you hit the next blocker, you know that, oh, maybe my screen has no data.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Then you start thinking, well, OK, how do I populate the data, you know, without actually manually enter it as a user?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so then you're starting to look at the data layer and maybe your data comes from a database.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And of course, one simple thing you could do is just place a database on the disk.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But I would.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I would recommend going one layer above and whatever is reading from the database and swapping out that layer.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So we have a layer of what we call a repository layer or data providers.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: They have different names.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so then, you know, you end up setting up that state.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And actually, a lot of I think a lot of state setup happens kind of in more modern applications happens at that level.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Then you might hit some APIs, you know, and you might you might see, oh, like this, this screen is failing because this API call happened and it, you know, it didn't return.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: By the way, another thing I would recommend doing is shutting off internet on your emulator when you're doing this so that you could actually make sure that your test is hermetic and it's not reaching out, not depending on network state.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so you're going to just hit all these different cases until you finally manage to set up all your dependencies in a way that launches that screen directly and makes it
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: you know show you the data that it wants and then from there i think it's actually very straightforward to use espresso uh to just manipulate a few um you know the settings or check things on the screen so really i think i think the most most work happens at the the level of uh setting up yeah and then you do this for a couple screens you're going to notice certain patterns you're going to notice oh i'm always um you know setting up this class
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: um and doing this thing and you're going to start seeing duplicate code and at that point i i would recommend that you start thinking about a new layer of abstraction and kind of hiding all of that difficult test setup in in some sort of an api what we call power pack
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So, so that's, that's kind of my recommendation, very hands-on, you know, kind of pragmatic way.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Excellent.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, definitely a lot of steps involved.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I've felt the same exact situations that you've recommended many times, just starting with that one simple screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And like, as you said, you kind of rewind from there and it's like, all right, well, how do I even get to, to launch the screen?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Where does that data come from?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you kind of have to solve each one of those problems before you can kind of get to a more stable environment.

text: So very good.

speaker: SPEAKER_01
